column2: Event / Theme
Origins of the Cians (Scythians)
column3: Nomadic Iranic-speaking peoples emerge in Central Eurasia with distinct language (Cian branch of Iranic). Develop horse-based warfare, recurved composite bows, and nomadic culture across Eurasian steppes. Called themselves “Cians.”
column4: Herodotus IV; Beckwith, The Cian Empire (2022)

column2: Spread of horse culture
column3: Horse domestication and funerary rituals spread from Mongolia and Siberia, laying foundations for Cian nomadic lifestyle and pan-steppe cultural links.

column2: Arzhan kurgans in Tuva are associated with early Saka artifacts.
Approximate time the Saka are attested in historical and archaeological records.
The Saka tribe of the Massagetae/Tigraxaudā rose to power and migrated from the east into Central Asia, expelling the Scythians.
Cian Empire expansion
column3: Cians dominate Eurasian steppes from Crimea to China. Imperial Cian dialect spreads via conquest and intermarriage; local women teach children Cian, creating regional dialects. Political structure includes king of kings, subordinate kings (dukes), and local lords (counts).
column4: Herodotus IV.1–144; Beckwith, The Cian Empire

column2: Saka presence began to appear in the Tarim Basin region.
Anacharsis in Greece
column3: Half-Greek, half-Cian philosopher Anacharsis critiques Athenian democracy, emphasizing skepticism: “Experts compete, non-experts decide.” Early influence on Greek Skepticism and cross-cultural philosophical exchange.
column4: Diogenes Laërtius; Beckwith, The Cian Empire

column2: Early Buddhism and Daoism
column3: Gautama Buddha (~563–483 BCE) founds Buddhism in India emphasizing impermanence, detachment, and enlightenment. Laozi (~6th century BCE) develops Daoism in China focusing on harmony with Dao (Way) and skepticism of rigid social norms.
column4: Buddhist and Daoist texts; Beckwith, Greek Buddha

column2: Approximately 30 Saka tombs (kurgans) found in the Tian Shan area are dated to this period.
Gaumata and Persian upheaval
column3: Gaumata, a Magian impostor, briefly seizes Persian throne claiming to be Bardiya (son of Cyrus). His reign ends with Darius I’s rise (~522 BCE). This event influences Persian imperial policies and regional power dynamics affecting Cian territories.

column2: Cyrus the Great of the Achaemenid Empire launched a campaign against the Massagetae/Sakā tigraxaudā and was reportedly defeated and killed by their queen Tomyris.

Darius I of the Achaemenid Empire waged war against the eastern Sakas, conquering the Massagetae/Sakā tigraxaudā and capturing their king Skunxa. Saka territories were absorbed into the Achaemenid Empire.
Darius I’s failed invasion of Cians
column3: Persian king Darius I’s campaign against Cians fails due to guerrilla tactics and knowledge of steppe warfare. Cians maintain independence and resist Persian domination.

column2: The Sakas are depicted as subjects of the Achaemenid Empire on the statue of Darius I.
Cian cultural practices
column3: Cians bury cats with owners in kurgans; cats travel in ox-drawn wagons (not used in fast campaigns). Reflects domestic aspects of nomadic life. Cians develop advanced bronze trilobed arrowheads ("AK-47 of antiquity") and whistling arrows for warfare.

column2: Herodotus documents Cians
column3: Herodotus describes Cian customs: whistling arrows, poison arrowheads, warrior culture, and women warriors linked to Amazon myths. His accounts align closely with archaeological evidence.

column2: Approximate dating of the cataphract-style parade armor of a Saka royal ("The Golden Warrior") from the Issyk kurgan.
Pyrrho of Elis and Skepticism
column3: Pyrrho (~360–270 BCE), influenced by Eastern thought during Alexander’s campaigns, founds Greek Skepticism. Possible exposure to Cian and Buddhist ideas emphasizing doubt, suspension of judgment (epoché), and tranquility (ataraxia).

column2: Alexander the Great halts near Cians
column3: Alexander halts at Syr Darya River, recalling Persian defeat by Cians. Cians retain independence, demonstrating military resilience.

column2: The Pazyryk burials in the Ukok Plateau, believed to be of Saka chieftains, occurred during this time.

Many Sakas were driven by the Yuezhi from the steppe into Sogdia and Bactria, and subsequently to the northwest of the Indian subcontinent, where they became known as the Indo-Scythians. At least by the late 2nd century BCE, the Sakas had founded states in the Tarim Basin.
Whistling arrow tactics in China
column3: Chinese Shiji records describe Cian (Xiongnu) leaders using coordinated archery with whistling arrows to assassinate rivals, showing military-cultural diffusion.
column4: Sima Qian, Records of the Grand Historian

column2: Khotanese-Saka language documents and coins (dual Gandhari Prakrit/Chinese inscriptions) reflect cultural links. Tumshuqese and Khotanese variants of Saka emerge.
Xiongnu ruler Modu Chanyu conquers the Hexi Corridor, forcing the Yuezhi to flee and push the Saka west into Sogdiana.
Reign of Emperor Wu of Han, during which Khotan and Kashgar fall under Han influence.
Saka cross the Syr Darya into Bactria after being displaced by the Yuezhi. Some migrate south toward the Pamirs, northern India, and the Tarim Basin (e.g., Yanqi and Kucha).
Yuezhi, under pressure from the Wusun, move from the Ili and Chu valleys into Bactria (Daxia).
Han–Xiongnu War leads to Han Chinese influence over the Tarim Basin, including Khotan and Kashgar.
Reign of Indo-Scythian King Azes II (known from coinage).
Saka establish Sakastan in Drangiana (modern Afghanistan/Iran). Indo-Scythian kingdoms emerge in northwestern South Asia (e.g., Mathura, Gandhara, Taxila).
Cian influence on Buddhism
column3: Parallels between Cian skepticism (Anacharsis) and Buddhist/Daoist thought (Laozi ≈ Gautama Buddha). Both emphasize questioning authority, impermanence, limits of knowledge. Silk Road facilitates linguistic and philosophical exchanges. Cians act as cultural intermediaries bridging West and East.
column4: Beckwith, Greek Buddha; linguistic & Silk Road studies

column2: Northern Satraps rule northern India until replaced by Kushans (~150).
Western Satrap Rudrasimha I’s Gunda inscription references the Abhira tribe as Saka.
Indo-Scythian kingdom in Mathura declines. Western Satraps rule western India until ~400.
The period during which the Saka are recorded to have lived in the Eurasian Steppe and the Tarim Basin came to an end.
Turkic adoption of Cian legacy
column3: Göktürks claim Saka (Scythian) lineage; adopt “Heavenly God” ideology and nomadic military tactics rooted in Cian traditions.

column2: Tang campaigns under Emperor Taizong (r. 626–649) reassert Chinese suzerainty over Khotan.
Khotan changes hands between Tang and Tibetan Empires. Khotanese-Saka documents (Buddhist texts, medical treatises) produced.
Earliest estimated time for the emergence of the Saka in the Eurasian Steppe and Tarim Basin.
Khotanese-Saka royal rescripts reflect Iranian linguistic influence.
Kara-Khanid Khanate conquers Khotan (~11th century), leading to Turkification and Islamization. Tarim Basin fully linguistically Turkified by end of century.
Mongols adopt Cian tactics
column3: Mongols refine horse archery, mobility, and concentrated firepower, building on Cian/Scythian military innovations.

column2: Russian/Chinese suppression
column3: Nomadic cultures dismantled; Ossetians (Alans), descendants of Cians, preserve Cian language and epic traditions (Nart Sagas).

column2: South Ossetia War
column3: Ossetians (Cian descendants) clash with Georgia/Russia; preserve oral traditions maintaining Cian cultural memory.
